title: Abnormal psychology: treatment of disorders
summary: How is one disorder treated, and how do we know whether the treatment actually works? Using major depressive disorder (MDD) as the worked case, this lesson builds three treatment approaches — biological (SSRIs), cognitive/psychological (CBT) and sociocultural (group and community support) — then confronts the hard part examiners reward: assessing effectiveness when relapse, placebo effects and individual differences muddy the picture, the role of culture, and the interactionist case for combined treatment. Every claim is tied to a named study or meta-analysis, and a full Paper 2 essay is marked against the real 22-mark markband.
learningObjectives: Explain one biological, one psychological and one sociocultural treatment for major depressive disorder, linking each to a proposed cause (etiology).
Use named studies and meta-analyses as evidence for and against each treatment — not just describe them.
Evaluate the effectiveness of treatments and explain why effectiveness is genuinely hard to measure (relapse, placebo, spontaneous remission, individual differences).
Discuss the role of culture in treatment and evaluate the interactionist (combined) approach.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Fixing the problem vs. proving you fixed it
simpleExplanation.summary: Treating a disorder has two halves. The first is choosing a treatment that matches a proposed cause. The second — the one students forget — is proving the treatment, and not something else, is what made the person better. Most of the marks live in that second half.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine someone recovers from a bad cold a week after starting a herbal tea. Did the tea cure them, or would they have recovered anyway? Maybe they got better simply because they believed the tea would work (placebo), or because colds pass on their own (spontaneous remission). Treating depression raises exactly the same problem: people improve for many reasons at once, so showing that the SSRI or the therapy caused the improvement requires careful control groups, not just a happy patient.
steps: Pick a treatment and link it to a proposed cause of MDD (e.g. SSRIs to the serotonin hypothesis; CBT to Beck's negative schemas).
Support it with a named study or meta-analysis — state aim, procedure and findings.
Evaluate effectiveness AND the difficulty of measuring it: relapse, placebo, spontaneous remission, individual differences, culture.
Weigh the interactionist option: combining biological and psychological treatment often outperforms either alone.

flashcards.back: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors block the reuptake transporter on the presynaptic neuron, so released serotonin stays in the synapse longer and binds more to postsynaptic receptors. They increase serotonin AVAILABILITY — they do not create more serotonin. Example: fluoxetine (Prozac).
The theory that low serotonin activity contributes to depression. SSRIs are the treatment that matches this proposed cause — always link treatment to etiology for top knowledge marks.

Cognitive Behavioural Therapy targets COGNITIONS (and behaviours), not brain chemistry. It teaches patients to identify and challenge negative automatic thoughts and maladaptive schemas (Beck's negative cognitive triad: negative views of self, world and future) and to test them against evidence.

Depressed thinking is organised around negative schemas about the SELF ('I am worthless'), the WORLD ('everything is against me') and the FUTURE ('nothing will improve'). CBT's job is to make these beliefs testable and then disconfirm them.

Sociocultural treatment: therapist-led group therapy or community support groups reduce isolation, normalise the disorder ('universality'), build social support and are low-cost and scalable. Weakness: exposure/sharing can clash with cultural norms of privacy.

Return of symptoms after apparent recovery. A treatment that works short-term but has high relapse (common with SSRIs once stopped) is not truly effective long-term. CBT tends to have LOWER relapse because it teaches transferable coping skills.

Improvement produced by the EXPECTATION of treatment rather than the treatment's active ingredient. Because a large share of antidepressant response is matched by placebo, a treatment must beat placebo — not just beat nothing — to be judged effective.

Improvement that occurs without treatment or despite it. Depression is often episodic and self-limiting, so some 'recovered' patients would have improved anyway — a key reason a no-treatment control group is needed.

Severity, motivation, verbal ability, comorbidity, culture and genetics all change how well a treatment works. An 'average' effect from a trial can hide huge variation, so a treatment effective for the group may fail a given patient.

Combining treatments from different levels of analysis (e.g. SSRIs + CBT). The biomedical model reduces symptoms quickly so the patient can engage in therapy, and therapy builds skills that lower relapse — combined treatment often outperforms either alone.

EFFICACY = does it work under tightly controlled trial conditions? EFFECTIVENESS = does it work in messy real-world practice (missed doses, comorbidity, dropout)? A treatment can have high efficacy but lower real-world effectiveness.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: describe vs USE research
flashcards.back: Retelling a study's method and findings is DESCRIBING. USING it means stating the study, then explicitly saying what it PROVES or QUESTIONS about the treatment ('...which supports SSRIs for severe depression BUT not mild, because...'). Only USED research reaches the top of Criterion C.

quickCheck.prompt: How do SSRIs actually change serotonin activity?
options: They cause the brain to produce more serotonin
They block reuptake so released serotonin stays in the synapse longer
They destroy serotonin receptors to reduce demand
They convert dopamine into serotonin
quickCheck.answer: They block reuptake so released serotonin stays in the synapse longer

quickCheck.prompt: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for depression primarily targets:
options: Serotonin levels in the synapse
Negative automatic thoughts and maladaptive schemas
Family inheritance of depression genes
The patient's diet and sleep hormones
quickCheck.answer: Negative automatic thoughts and maladaptive schemas

quickCheck.prompt: A trial finds patients improved on an SSRI. Why can't we conclude the drug alone caused the improvement?
options: Because SSRIs are always ineffective
Because placebo effects, spontaneous remission and individual differences could also explain improvement
Because depression cannot be measured at all
Because only CBT can cause real improvement
quickCheck.answer: Because placebo effects, spontaneous remission and individual differences could also explain improvement

quickCheck.prompt: In a Paper 2 essay, which of these is USING research rather than merely DESCRIBING it?
options: 'Elkin et al. tested imipramine, CBT and placebo on depressed patients.'
'The sample was large and the study lasted 16 weeks.'
'Elkin et al.'s finding that the drug matched CBT for severe cases supports SSRIs — but only for severe depression, which limits the claim that they work for everyone.'
'Many studies have looked at depression treatments over the years.'
quickCheck.answer: 'Elkin et al.'s finding that the drug matched CBT for severe cases supports SSRIs — but only for severe depression, which limits the claim that they work for everyone.'

quickCheck.prompt: Why does CBT tend to show LOWER relapse rates than SSRIs once treatment stops?
options: Because CBT changes the patient's genes permanently
Because CBT teaches transferable coping and thought-challenging skills the patient keeps using
Because CBT works faster in the first week
Because CBT has no individual differences in outcome
quickCheck.answer: Because CBT teaches transferable coping and thought-challenging skills the patient keeps using

faq.q: The question says 'two treatments for ONE disorder' — do both treatments have to be from different approaches?
faq.a: No requirement that they be from different approaches, but it is the smart move. Choosing one biological (SSRIs) and one psychological (CBT) gives you a natural axis of contrast — speed vs. relapse, symptoms vs. causes, side-effects vs. dropout — which fuels the 'developed' critical thinking Criterion D wants. Two treatments from the same approach make genuine evaluation harder.

faq.q: Do I need to name specific drugs and studies?
faq.a: Yes, specificity earns marks. Name the drug class (SSRI) and one example (fluoxetine/Prozac), and name your studies (e.g. Elkin et al., 1989; Kirsch et al., 2008; March et al., 2007). Vague references — 'studies have shown' — cannot reach the top of Criterion C (Use of research).

faq.q: Is it a problem that I can't remember exact percentages from a study?
faq.a: Not fatal. Examiners reward accurate understanding of a study's AIM, PROCEDURE and FINDINGS and, above all, how you USE it. Approximate figures ('roughly 40% of placebo patients also improved') are fine if the direction and meaning are right. A precise number used only to describe scores lower than a rough number used to build an argument.

faq.q: Can one study support the etiology AND the treatment?
faq.a: Yes, and doing so shows sophistication. A trial showing SSRIs relieve depression is evidence both for the serotonin hypothesis (cause) and for drug therapy (treatment). But beware the treatment-etiology fallacy: SSRIs working does not prove low serotonin caused the depression, any more than aspirin curing a headache proves the headache was caused by low aspirin.

sections.content: This lesson answers two questions about one disorder — major depressive disorder (MDD). First: how is it treated? We build three approaches — biological (SSRIs), cognitive/psychological (CBT) and sociocultural (group and community support). Second, and this is where Paper 2 marks are won: how do we know whether a treatment actually works? Depression lifts for many reasons at once — the drug, the belief that the drug will help, the natural passing of a low episode — so proving that the treatment caused the recovery is genuinely hard. We will tie every claim to a named study, confront the measurement problem head-on, weigh the role of culture, and finish by marking a full 22-mark essay against the real IB markband.
The disorder and the etiology–treatment link

Major depressive disorder involves persistent low mood, loss of interest or pleasure (anhedonia), disturbed sleep and appetite, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness and, in severe cases, suicidal ideation, lasting at least two weeks. The golden rule for treatment questions is that every treatment should be linked to a proposed CAUSE. Biological treatments assume a physical cause (low serotonin activity); cognitive treatments assume a cognitive cause (negative schemas); sociocultural treatments assume that isolation and social context maintain the disorder. Match treatment to etiology and your knowledge marks look deliberate rather than accidental.

The biological approach treats MDD as an illness with a physical basis. The most common biological treatment is drug therapy with Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs), such as fluoxetine (Prozac). These match the serotonin hypothesis of depression — the theory that reduced serotonin activity contributes to low mood.

items: **Mechanism:** After a neuron releases serotonin into the synapse, a reuptake transporter normally pumps it back into the presynaptic neuron. SSRIs block that transporter, so serotonin lingers in the synapse and binds more to postsynaptic receptors. They raise serotonin AVAILABILITY — a common exam error is writing that they 'produce more serotonin'.
**Onset:** Symptom relief typically takes 2–6 weeks, even though the chemical effect is immediate — a puzzle for the simple serotonin story.
**Effectiveness:** Reliable for moderate-to-severe MDD; benefit over placebo is small for mild depression (Kirsch et al., 2008).
**Side-effects:** Nausea, insomnia, agitation, sexual dysfunction; a boxed warning about increased suicidal ideation in adolescents.
**Relapse:** High once medication stops, because the drug treats symptoms, not the thinking patterns or life circumstances behind them.

sections.content: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy matches a cognitive etiology: Beck argued that depression is maintained by negative SCHEMAS organised into a negative cognitive triad — negative beliefs about the self ('I am worthless'), the world ('everything is against me') and the future ('nothing will get better'). These schemas generate biased, automatic negative thoughts that keep mood low. CBT's core move is to make those beliefs explicit, treat them as hypotheses rather than facts, and test them against evidence (cognitive restructuring), alongside behavioural activation — scheduling rewarding activity to break the withdrawal–low-mood cycle.

items: **Target:** Cognitions and behaviours — NOT brain chemistry. This is the clean contrast with SSRIs and the source of most evaluation points.
**Method:** Identify negative automatic thoughts, challenge distortions (catastrophising, overgeneralising), set behavioural experiments, build coping skills.
**Effectiveness:** Comparable to medication for moderate depression, with markedly LOWER relapse because the skills outlast the therapy (Hollon et al.; Butler et al., 2006 meta-analysis).
**Limitations:** Demands motivation, verbal fluency and effort; less accessible to severely depressed or non-verbal patients; therapist quality varies; slower initial relief than drugs.

sections.content: Sociocultural treatment: group and community support

The sociocultural approach treats isolation and social context as maintainers of depression, so it uses the group as the healing agent. Group CBT and community support groups bring together people with similar difficulties under (or without) a therapist's guidance.

items: **Mechanism:** 'Universality' — realising others share the problem reduces shame and isolation; members model coping and give mutual support; the group normalises help-seeking.
**Access and cost:** One therapist reaches many patients, making it scalable and affordable — important where clinicians are scarce.
**Evidence:** Bright et al. (1999) found group therapy (both cognitive-behavioural and mutual-support formats) reduced depressive symptoms, showing the group format itself has therapeutic value.
**Limitations:** Sharing personal feelings in a group can clash with cultural norms of privacy or 'saving face'; less individualised; group dynamics can occasionally reinforce negative thinking.

sections.question: Explain one biological treatment for major depressive disorder, with reference to one study. [9 marks]
sections.solution:
One biological treatment for major depressive disorder (MDD) is drug therapy using Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs), such as fluoxetine. This treatment matches the serotonin hypothesis of depression — the proposed cause that reduced serotonin activity contributes to low mood. Mechanistically, when a neuron releases serotonin into the synapse, a reuptake transporter normally reabsorbs it into the presynaptic neuron. SSRIs block this transporter, so serotonin remains in the synapse longer and binds more to postsynaptic receptors, increasing serotonin availability and, over 2–6 weeks, relieving symptoms.

Elkin et al. (1989) provides supporting evidence. AIM: to compare the effectiveness of drug treatment (imipramine), CBT and a placebo for depression. PROCEDURE: a large multi-site randomised clinical trial (the NIMH Treatment of Depression Collaborative Research Program) randomly allocated depressed patients to conditions and tracked symptoms over 16 weeks. FINDINGS: for SEVERE depression the drug was as effective as CBT and more effective than placebo, though a substantial share of placebo patients also improved.

USING this: the study supports SSRIs as an effective biological treatment for severe MDD, and the strong placebo response shows WHY a controlled comparison — not just 'the patient got better' — is needed to credit the drug. However, effectiveness is limited: SSRIs relieve symptoms without addressing thoughts or circumstances, so relapse is high once medication stops, and side-effects (nausea, insomnia, sexual dysfunction) reduce adherence. SSRIs are therefore effective but incomplete.

sections.content: Assessing effectiveness — and why it is so hard to measure
This is the section that separates top essays. It is not enough to say a treatment 'works'; you must show you understand why proving it works is difficult. Four problems recur.

items: **Placebo.** Much of an antidepressant's apparent benefit is matched by placebo. Kirsch et al. (2008) meta-analysed published AND unpublished antidepressant trials submitted to the US FDA and found the drug–placebo difference was small and clinically negligible for mild-to-moderate depression, becoming meaningful only in severe cases. So 'the patient improved on the drug' does not prove the DRUG worked.

**Spontaneous remission.** Depression is often episodic and self-limiting; some patients would recover anyway. Without a no-treatment control you cannot separate the treatment's effect from natural recovery.

**Relapse.** Short-term symptom relief is not long-term effectiveness. SSRIs show high relapse once stopped; CBT shows lower relapse because skills persist. Which treatment 'works better' depends on the time window you measure.

**Individual differences.** Severity, comorbidity, motivation, verbal ability, genetics and culture all move outcomes. An average trial effect can hide huge variation, so a treatment that is 'effective' on average can fail a specific patient.

**Measurement itself.** Depression is assessed with self-report scales (e.g. the Beck Depression Inventory) vulnerable to social desirability and demand characteristics — outcomes are estimates, not readings.

sections.question: A newspaper reports: 'New study proves antidepressants work — 60% of patients recovered within three months.' Using what you know about measuring effectiveness, explain two reasons this headline overstates the evidence. [Worked reasoning]
sections.solution:
Reason 1 — no control group / spontaneous remission. A 60% recovery rate is meaningless without a comparison. Depression is often episodic and self-limiting, so some of those patients would have recovered in three months WITHOUT any drug. Only comparing the drug group to a no-treatment or placebo group isolates the drug's specific effect. Kirsch et al. (2008) matters here: when antidepressants were compared with placebo across all trials (including unpublished ones), the difference was small except in severe depression — so a raw recovery figure with no placebo comparison tells us almost nothing about the drug itself.

Reason 2 — placebo effect and expectation. Even if there were a placebo group, a large share of placebo patients typically improve simply because they EXPECT to get better. So 'recovered on the drug' conflates the chemical effect with the belief effect. A responsible claim would report the drug–placebo DIFFERENCE, not the headline recovery rate. (A strong answer could add a third point: 60% 'recovery' depends entirely on how recovery was measured — a self-report scale is open to social desirability — and says nothing about RELAPSE after three months.)

This is exactly the reasoning examiners reward under critical thinking: not 'the drug is bad', but a precise account of WHY the evidence does not support the strong claim.

sections.content: The role of culture in treatment

Most mainstream treatments — SSRIs, CBT — were developed and validated in Western, individualist contexts, so their effectiveness cannot be assumed to be universal. Culture shapes how distress is expressed (in many cultures depression presents as bodily symptoms such as fatigue or pain rather than reported sadness), what counts as an acceptable treatment, and whether talking openly about private feelings is appropriate at all.

items: **Cultural mismatch.** Kinzie et al. (1987) found Southeast Asian refugee patients responded poorly to therapy requiring open disclosure of personal feelings and had low adherence even to medication — a mismatch between a Western therapeutic model and the patients' cultural norms.

**Culturally adapted CBT works better.** Adapting CBT to local language, idioms of distress and values (rather than importing it unchanged) improves outcomes — evidence that the therapy is not universally 'plug-and-play'.

**Somatic presentation.** If a culture expresses depression as physical complaints, a clinician using a Western symptom checklist may misdiagnose or misjudge severity, distorting any effectiveness measure.

**Etic vs. emic caution.** Applying a Western treatment as if it were culture-free (an imposed etic) risks both poor effectiveness and ethical problems around informed, culturally-appropriate consent.

sections.content: Because MDD has biological, cognitive and social contributors, an interactionist (eclectic) approach combines treatments across levels of analysis rather than betting on one. The logic is complementary: medication can reduce severe symptoms quickly enough that a patient becomes able to engage in therapy, while CBT builds the thinking and coping skills that lower relapse after the drug stops.

items: **Evidence:** March et al. (2007), the Treatment for Adolescents with Depression Study (TADS), compared fluoxetine alone, CBT alone, their COMBINATION, and placebo in depressed adolescents. The combination produced the best response rate, outperforming either treatment on its own.
**Why it works:** drugs act fast on symptoms; therapy acts durably on causes and relapse — combining them covers each treatment's weakness.

**Evaluation:** combined treatment is more expensive and demanding, and is not automatically best for every patient — mild cases may do fine on CBT alone, and adding a drug means adding side-effects. Interactionism is a strong DEFAULT, not a universal rule.

sections.content: In a treatment ERQ, link every treatment to a proposed CAUSE (SSRIs to the serotonin hypothesis; CBT to negative schemas). This does double duty: it earns Criterion B (Knowledge) for showing WHY the treatment targets what it targets, and it sets up Criterion D (Critical thinking) via the treatment-etiology fallacy — the reminder that a treatment working does not prove the cause is its mirror image.

items: **Writing that SSRIs 'produce more serotonin'.** They block REUPTAKE, increasing availability of existing serotonin — the wrong mechanism signals shaky knowledge.

**Saying CBT changes brain chemistry.** CBT targets COGNITIONS and behaviours (negative schemas), not neurotransmitters. Mixing up the mechanisms collapses the very contrast your evaluation depends on.

**Treating 'the patient got better' as proof the treatment worked.** Without controlling for placebo, spontaneous remission and individual differences, improvement proves nothing. Effectiveness is HARD to measure — say so.
**Describing studies instead of USING them.** Retelling aim/procedure/findings is Criterion C's floor, not its ceiling. You must state what the study PROVES or QUESTIONS about the treatment.
**Answering the wrong number of treatments.** 'Evaluate two treatments' requires TWO, both evaluated. One treatment richly evaluated still caps your marks because half the question is unaddressed.

**All description, no evaluation.** An essay that only explains how treatments work — with no effectiveness evidence, side-effects or methodological critique — cannot reach the upper critical-thinking bands, however accurate.
**Assuming Western treatments are universal.** Ignoring culture (mismatch, somatic presentation, need for adaptation) misses easy, high-value critical-thinking marks.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB Paper 2 markband (out of 22)

sections.question: Evaluate two treatments for one disorder. [22 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model essay**

**Thesis (Focus).** Major depressive disorder (MDD) can be treated biologically with SSRIs and cognitively with CBT. This essay argues that both are genuinely effective for moderate-to-severe MDD, but each is effective in a different sense and along a different time-frame — SSRIs relieve symptoms fast yet relapse when stopped, while CBT works more slowly but durably — and that a fair evaluation depends less on which 'wins' than on recognising how hard effectiveness is to measure. This tension points toward combining them.

**Treatment 1 — SSRIs (Knowledge).** SSRIs such as fluoxetine match the serotonin hypothesis of MDD. After a neuron releases serotonin, a reuptake transporter normally reabsorbs it; SSRIs block that transporter, so serotonin stays in the synapse longer and binds more to postsynaptic receptors, raising serotonin availability and, over several weeks, lifting mood. They reduce symptoms quickly relative to therapy but do not address the thoughts or circumstances behind the depression.

**Research USED for SSRIs.** Elkin et al. (1989) aimed to compare imipramine, CBT and placebo in depressed patients in a large multi-site randomised trial over 16 weeks. For SEVERE depression the drug matched CBT and beat placebo. This is used, not just reported: it supports SSRIs for severe MDD, yet the substantial placebo improvement in the same study shows that raw recovery cannot be credited to the drug — a controlled comparison is essential. Kirsch et al. (2008) sharpens the point: meta-analysing published AND unpublished FDA-submitted trials, they found the drug–placebo difference was small for mild-to-moderate depression and clinically meaningful only for severe cases. Used together, these studies support a NARROW claim — SSRIs are effective for severe MDD — while undermining the broad claim that they work for everyone.

**Evaluation of SSRIs (Critical thinking, developed).** Strengths: fast, accessible, scalable, strong for severe cases. But three developed limitations follow from the evidence above. (1) Effectiveness is inflated by the placebo effect, so much of the apparent benefit is expectation, not chemistry (Kirsch). (2) Relapse is high once medication stops, because symptoms — not causes — are treated, so short-term efficacy overstates long-term effectiveness. (3) Side-effects (nausea, insomnia, sexual dysfunction, and raised suicidal ideation in adolescents) reduce adherence and raise ethical concerns. There is also the treatment-etiology fallacy: SSRIs relieving symptoms does not prove low serotonin CAUSED the depression, so the drug's success cannot validate the serotonin hypothesis.

**Treatment 2 — CBT (Knowledge).** CBT matches a cognitive etiology: Beck's negative cognitive triad, in which depression is maintained by negative schemas about the self, world and future. CBT makes these automatic thoughts explicit, treats them as testable hypotheses and disconfirms them through cognitive restructuring and behavioural activation. It targets cognitions and behaviours — not neurotransmitters — which is precisely why it can produce durable change.

**Research USED for CBT.** Butler et al. (2006) meta-analysed CBT outcome studies and found large effect sizes for depression, with benefits maintained after therapy ended. Used, not described: this supports CBT as effective AND explains its key advantage over SSRIs — lower relapse — because the skills outlast the treatment, directly addressing the long-term weakness of drug therapy identified above.

**Evaluation of CBT (Critical thinking, developed).** Strengths: comparable to medication for moderate depression, no pharmacological side-effects, and lower relapse because coping skills persist. Limitations: it demands motivation, effort and verbal ability, so severely depressed or less verbal patients may be unable to engage; therapist quality varies; and initial relief is slower than with drugs. Crucially, CBT was developed in a Western individualist context — Kinzie et al. (1987) found Southeast Asian patients responded poorly to therapy requiring open emotional disclosure, so effectiveness is not culture-free and unadapted CBT can mismatch a patient's norms.

**The measurement problem (Critical thinking).** Comparing the two treatments is complicated by how effectiveness is measured. Spontaneous remission means some patients recover anyway; placebo effects inflate drug outcomes; individual differences mean average trial results hide wide variation; and self-report scales are open to social desirability. So 'which treatment works better' is not a single fact but depends on severity, time-frame and how outcome is measured.

**Conclusion (Focus).** Both SSRIs and CBT are effective for moderate-to-severe MDD, but in different senses: SSRIs offer rapid symptom relief with high relapse and side-effects, while CBT offers slower but more durable, side-effect-free change. Because their strengths are complementary, the strongest evidence favours COMBINING them — March et al. (2007, TADS) found fluoxetine plus CBT outperformed either alone in adolescents. The honest verdict is therefore not that one treatment is best, but that effectiveness is conditional and hardest to prove precisely where the headlines are loudest.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22)**

- **A — Focus on the question (2/2).** The thesis names the disorder and the two treatments and states a defensible argument (each effective in a different sense/time-frame); the conclusion returns to it. The command term 'evaluate' is delivered — not just an explanation.
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6).** Both treatments are explained accurately and in depth, each LINKED to an etiology (SSRIs↔serotonin hypothesis; CBT↔negative cognitive triad), with correct mechanisms and appropriate terminology (reuptake, availability, schemas, behavioural activation).
- **C — Use of research (6/6 — USED, not just described).** Elkin (1989), Kirsch (2008), Butler (2006), Kinzie (1987) and March (2007) are each deployed to make or qualify a specific claim ('supports SSRIs for SEVERE depression BUT undermines the broad claim...'). This is the top-band difference: research is integrated into the argument, not parked in a paragraph.
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6 — DEVELOPED evaluation).** Evaluation is developed rather than listed: effectiveness evidence, side-effects, relapse over time, placebo/spontaneous remission, the measurement problem, culture, and the treatment-etiology fallacy — each explained, not merely named, and used to compare the two treatments.
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2).** Structured thesis → treatment 1 → treatment 2 → measurement → conclusion; each point flows into the next; consistent terminology.

**Why the top bands, specifically:** Criterion C reaches the top only because studies are USED to develop the argument (they change what we can claim), and Criterion D reaches the top only because the evaluation is DEVELOPED — each critique is explained and connected — rather than a bullet list of strengths and weaknesses. A response with the same studies merely SUMMARISED, and the same criticisms merely LISTED, would sit a full band lower on both C and D.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to 'Evaluate two treatments for one disorder [22]', submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official Paper 2 markband, telling you the band you reached on each criterion and why. Watch the two caps that quietly sink strong-sounding essays: (1) DESCRIBING research instead of USING it caps Criterion C — you must state what each study proves or questions; and (2) an essay that only EXPLAINS how treatments work, with no developed evaluation, caps Criterion D. The habits that lift you over both caps: after every study write 'which shows/questions...', and after every treatment write a developed 'but'.
